cards.q: Why does intact normal skin resist bacterial infection? List the factors that enhance this resistance.
cards.lecturer: Bacteria exist in the external environment, but the intact normal skin has a strong resistance to bacterial multiplication and invasion. The factors that enhance this resistance include: (i) relative dryness of normal skin, (ii) low skin pH of 5.5, (iii) unsaturated long chain fatty acids in skin lipids, (iv) natural antibacterial substances in the sebum, (v) circulating immunoglobulins, (vi) cellular immunity, (vii) delayed hypersensitivity, and (viii) bacterial interference, which is the suppression of bacterial species or strains by other bacteria.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Start with the surprising fact

Your skin is **covered in bacteria** right now. Millions of them.

Yet you are not infected.

---

>> The question is not *"how do bacteria get in?"* The question is **"why don't they get in more often?"**

The eight factors are the answer.

---

## Step 2: Group them into three kinds of defence

Eight items is hard to remember. **Three groups is easy.**

---

### Group 1 — Physical and chemical (the surface itself)

**Relative dryness**

Bacteria need moisture to multiply. Dry skin is a **desert** to them.

>> This immediately explains why **increased humidity** is a risk factor, and why boils favour the **axilla and groin** — the moist places.

---

**Low pH of 5.5**

Skin is **acidic**. This is called the *acid mantle*.

Most pathogens prefer a neutral pH of around 7.

>> Harsh alkaline soaps raise skin pH — which is why over-washing can, paradoxically, make infection more likely.

---

**Unsaturated long chain fatty acids in skin lipids**

These fatty acids are **directly toxic** to many bacteria.

---

**Natural antibacterial substances in the sebum**

Sebum is not just grease. It carries **antimicrobial peptides**.

---

### Group 2 — Immune defences

**Circulating immunoglobulins** — antibodies reaching the skin from blood

**Cellular immunity** — T cells and phagocytes

**Delayed hypersensitivity** — the slower, T-cell-mediated response that walls off organisms

---

### Group 3 — Biological warfare

**Bacterial interference** — and this one is the most interesting.

---

## Step 3: What is bacterial interference?

>> "The **suppression of bacterial species or strains by other bacteria**."

The harmless bacteria already living on your skin actively fight off the dangerous ones.

They do it by:

- **Occupying the space** — no room for newcomers
- **Consuming the nutrients**
- **Producing substances** that kill competitors

---

>> Your normal skin flora is not passive decoration. It is a **standing army**.

This explains why prolonged antibiotics can lead to skin infection — you kill the friendly army and leave the territory undefended.

---

## Putting it all together

>> Skin is **dry, acidic, chemically hostile, immunologically patrolled, and already occupied**.

Infection only happens when one or more of those eight defences fails.

Which is exactly what the next card is about.
cards.tie:
This card is the foundation of the whole lecture.

Every risk factor in the next card is simply **one of these eight defences being knocked out**.

And every treatment is either restoring a defence or replacing it.

cards.q: What factors favour bacterial skin infections, and how does each one work?
cards.lecturer: Factors that favour bacterial skin infections include: (i) trauma (abrasions, scratches, cuts), (ii) insect bites, (iii) increased humidity, (iv) malnutrition, (v) severe illness, (vi) low standards of personal or community hygiene, (vii) coexisting skin disorders such as atopic dermatitis, (viii) natural or acquired immune depression, and (ix) iatrogenic immune suppression.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Do not memorise nine items. Match them to the defences.

>> Every risk factor **cancels one of the eight defences** from the previous card.

Once you see that, the list writes itself.

---

## Group 1 — They breach the physical barrier

**Trauma — abrasions, scratches, cuts**

The intact surface is gone. Bacteria walk in.

---

**Insect bites**

Two problems at once:

- The bite itself punctures the skin
- **The child scratches it**, widening the breach and carrying bacteria from under the fingernails

>> This is why insect bites are listed as a **predisposing lesion for impetigo** later in the lecture.

---

**Coexisting skin disorders — atopic dermatitis**

Eczematous skin is:

- **Cracked and fissured** — barrier broken
- **Itchy** — so it is scratched constantly
- Already **colonised heavily with _S. aureus_**

>> An eczematous child is a walking invitation to skin infection. This is also a listed risk factor for **cellulitis**.

---

## Group 2 — They change the environment

**Increased humidity**

Go back to defence number one: **relative dryness**.

Humidity cancels it directly. Wet skin lets bacteria multiply.

---

>> This is why our climate matters, and why **hyperhidrosis and maceration** appear again as predisposing factors for furuncles.

---

## Group 3 — They weaken the host

**Malnutrition**

A malnourished child cannot mount an immune response:

- Low immunoglobulins
- Impaired cellular immunity
- Poor skin integrity and healing

>> This is enormously relevant here. Malnutrition and skin sepsis travel together.

---

**Severe illness**

Any serious illness diverts resources and suppresses immunity.

---

**Natural or acquired immune depression**

"Natural" = congenital immunodeficiency.

"Acquired" = **HIV**, malignancy, diabetes.

---

**Iatrogenic immune suppression**

**Iatrogenic** = caused by medical treatment.

Steroids, chemotherapy, immunosuppressants.

>> Note that the lecturer separates *acquired* from *iatrogenic*. Both suppress immunity, but one we cause ourselves — and can therefore anticipate.

---

## Group 4 — They increase the bacterial load

**Low standards of personal or community hygiene**

>> Note the word **community**. This is not only about the individual child washing.

Overcrowding, shared towels, shared bedding, poor water supply — these spread organisms between children.

And this is exactly why impetigo *"may be spread to other parts of the body by the fingers, clothing, and towels"* — and to other children the same way.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Break the skin · wet the skin · weaken the child · raise the bacterial load.**

Four mechanisms. Nine risk factors. One outcome.
cards.tie:
This is why treatment of every infection in this lecture includes **personal hygiene** alongside antibiotics.

Antibiotics kill the organism present today.

Only correcting the risk factor stops the next one.

cards.q: Compare folliculitis, furunculosis and carbuncle. What is the relationship between them?
cards.lecturer: Folliculitis is the superficial inflammation and infection of a single hair follicle, appearing as small, itchy, pus-filled bumps. Furunculosis is the condition of having multiple, deeper, and more severe infections of the hair follicles known as boils (furuncles), which extend into subcutaneous tissue; the causative agent is usually S. aureus, which penetrates abraded perifollicular skin; the lesions may originate from a preceding folliculitis. Carbuncle is an infection of a group of contiguous follicles, with multiple drainage points, accompanied by inflammatory changes in surrounding connective tissue; it may be accompanied by fever, leukocytosis and bacteraemia.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: These are not three diseases. They are one disease, three depths.

>> This is the single most useful idea in the whole lecture.

Same organism — usually ***S. aureus***. Same starting point — the hair follicle.

The only difference is **how deep and how wide** it goes.

---

## Step 2: The progression

**FOLLICULITIS** — one follicle, **superficial**

↓ goes deeper

**FURUNCLE (boil)** — one follicle, into **subcutaneous tissue**

↓ spreads sideways

**CARBUNCLE** — a **group of contiguous** follicles

---

>> And the lecturer confirms the link explicitly: furuncles *"may originate from a **preceding folliculitis**"*.

---

## Step 3: Folliculitis — how to recognise it

Small, tender, red or pus-filled bumps, **clustered around hair follicles**.

**The giveaway sign:**

>> Each bump looks like a small pimple **with a hair sticking out of the centre**.

That one detail tells you the infection is *in the follicle* — and therefore that it is folliculitis, not something else.

**Sites:** face, scalp, thighs, buttocks — all hair-bearing.

---

## Step 4: Furuncle — follow the progression precisely

The lecturer gives an exact sequence. Learn it in order:

---

**1. Indurated lesion** — firm, hard swelling

**2. Central necrosis** — the middle dies

**3. Suppuration** — pus forms

**4. Rupture and discharge of a central core of necrotic tissue**

**5. Destruction of the follicle**

**6. Healing with scar formation**

---

>> **The "central core" is the classic feature.** A boil discharges a solid plug of dead tissue, not just pus. That is what people mean when they say a boil has "come to a head" and "pointed".

**And because the follicle is destroyed, it heals with a scar.** Folliculitis, being superficial, does not scar.

---

## Step 5: Where do boils occur, and why there?

**Scalp, face, back of neck, axillae, buttocks, groin.**

>> All **hair-bearing**. And notice: axillae, buttocks and groin are also **warm, moist and subject to friction**.

Which is exactly the predisposing factor list:

- **Obesity** — skin folds, moisture, friction
- **Hyperhidrosis** — excessive sweating
- **Maceration** — skin softened by moisture
- **Friction** — repeated rubbing damages the surface
- **Pre-existing dermatitis** — barrier already broken

>> Every one of them is either **moisture** or **breach of the surface**. The same two mechanisms from the risk factor card.

---

## Step 6: Carbuncle — why is it so much more serious?

Not one follicle. **A group of contiguous follicles**, with **multiple drainage points**.

Plus inflammatory changes in the **surrounding connective tissue**.

---

>> Picture it: several boils fused into one mass, discharging from several openings at once. Painful, tender, firm to hard, indurated.

---

**And now the child becomes systemically ill:**

- **Fever**
- **Leukocytosis**
- **Bacteraemia**

---

>> **Bacteraemia** means bacteria have entered the bloodstream. That is why a carbuncle needs **oral or parenteral** antibiotics, while folliculitis is often managed with topical treatment alone.

**Sites:** back of neck, shoulder, hip, thigh.

---

## Putting it all together

| | Follicles | Depth | Systemic illness | Scar |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Folliculitis** | One | Superficial | No | No |
| **Furuncle** | One | Subcutaneous | Sometimes | **Yes** |
| **Carbuncle** | **Group** | Deep + connective tissue | **Fever, leukocytosis, bacteraemia** | Yes |

>> One organism. Three depths. And the treatment escalates with the depth.
cards.tie:
This explains the escalation in treatment:

- Folliculitis → topical mupirocin/clindamycin
- Furuncle → antibiotics + **incision and drainage**
- Carbuncle → **oral or parenteral** antibiotics

The deeper it goes, the more systemic your treatment must be.

cards.q: How is each of folliculitis, furunculosis and carbuncle treated, and why does the treatment escalate?
cards.lecturer: Folliculitis: warm compresses; antibacterial washes (like benzoyl peroxide); topical antibiotics (e.g. mupirocin or clindamycin); severe or stubborn cases may require oral antibiotics (like cephalexin or doxycycline); avoid shaving, picking the bumps, and tight clothing to prevent spreading; good personal hygiene. Furunculosis: antibiotics, personal hygiene, hot moist compress, incision and drainage. Carbuncles: oral or parenteral antibiotics such as cloxacillin, cephalexin; cephalosporin, clindamycin, or erythromycin for penicillin allergic patients; in MRSA, rifampicin, cotrimoxazole; attention to personal hygiene.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The rule that governs all three

>> **The deeper the infection, the more systemic the treatment.**

Topical drugs cannot reach subcutaneous tissue. That is the whole logic.

---

## Step 2: Folliculitis — mostly topical

**Warm compresses**

Why? They **increase blood flow** to the area, bringing white cells and antibodies, and help the lesions drain.

---

**Antibacterial washes (benzoyl peroxide)**

Reduces the bacterial load on the surface — attacking the *source*, not just the lesion.

---

**Topical antibiotics — mupirocin or clindamycin**

The infection is **superficial**, so a topical drug can actually reach it.

---

**Oral antibiotics only if severe or stubborn** — cephalexin or doxycycline.

---

## Step 3: The advice that matters as much as the drugs

**Avoid shaving** — shaving causes micro-cuts around follicles and physically spreads organisms across the skin with the blade.

**Avoid picking** — this ruptures lesions and carries bacteria on the fingers to new follicles.

**Avoid tight clothing** — traps **moisture** and causes **friction**.

>> Look at that last one again. Moisture and friction are exactly the predisposing factors for furuncles. Tight clothing is quietly turning folliculitis into boils.

---

## Step 4: Furuncle — and why antibiotics alone will fail

The furuncle contains a **central core of necrotic tissue and pus**.

---

**Why can antibiotics not fix that?**

**Step 1:** Antibiotics travel in **blood**.

**Step 2:** An abscess cavity has **no blood supply inside it**.

**Step 3:** So the drug never reaches the pus at any useful concentration.

---

>> This is the oldest principle in surgery: **"Where there is pus, let it out."**

**Incision and drainage** physically removes what the antibiotic cannot reach.

---

**Hot moist compress** — encourages the boil to "point" and drain naturally, and increases local blood flow.

---

## Step 5: Carbuncle — systemic, because the infection is systemic

>> **Oral or parenteral** antibiotics. Not topical.

**Why?** Because the carbuncle involves deep tissue and the child may already have **fever, leukocytosis and bacteraemia**.

A cream on the surface is irrelevant to bacteria in the blood.

---

## Step 6: Learn the antibiotic choices as three situations

---

**Situation 1 — Ordinary _S. aureus_**

**Cloxacillin** or **cephalexin**.

>> Why these? Because *S. aureus* produces **beta-lactamase**, an enzyme that destroys ordinary penicillin. Cloxacillin is **beta-lactamase resistant** — it survives the enzyme.

---

**Situation 2 — Penicillin-allergic patient**

**Cephalosporin, clindamycin, or erythromycin.**

>> These are different drug classes, so they do not cross-react in the same way.

---

**Situation 3 — MRSA**

**MRSA** = Methicillin-Resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*.

It is resistant to **all** the beta-lactams — including cloxacillin and the cephalosporins.

>> So you must jump to an entirely different class: **rifampicin** or **cotrimoxazole**.

---

## Step 7: Personal hygiene appears in all three

This is not filler.

>> Antibiotics kill the organism causing **today's** lesion. Hygiene prevents **tomorrow's**.

And in furunculosis — which by definition means **recurrent, multiple** boils — preventing recurrence *is* the treatment.

---

## Putting it all together

>> Superficial → **topical**. Pus present → **drain it**. Systemic illness → **systemic antibiotics**.

And always ask what MRSA would change.
cards.tie:
The escalation mirrors the depth exactly.

And 'incision and drainage' for the furuncle is the principle you will use again for every abscess in medicine.

cards.q: Describe impetigo, and distinguish bullous from non-bullous impetigo.
cards.lecturer: Impetigo is a contagious bacterial infection of epidermis, transmitted by direct contact with infected persons or fomites. It can affect any skin region but is most common on the face around nose, mouth and hand, and commonly affects children aged 4–7 years. Primary impetigo is more common in children, with infection through minor breaks in the skin; secondary impetigo occurs at any age on secondary infection of trauma or wounds. Superficial blisters rupture easily, releasing yellow exudates that dry and form a honey-coloured crust. Bullous impetigo is always caused by S. aureus phage group 2, develops on intact skin, with vesicles and flaccid transparent bullae containing clear yellow or slightly turbid fluid, occurring more on face, buttocks, trunk, perineum and extremities; it is common in neonates and children under 5 years and systemic symptoms are common. Non-bullous impetigo is more common, usually due to S. aureus or S. pyogenes or mixed infection, typically affects trauma sites, and presents as scattered discrete 1–3 cm lesions with honey-coloured crust and surrounding erythema.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Where does impetigo sit?

>> **The epidermis.** The most superficial infection in this lecture.

That single fact explains almost everything about it.

---

**Because it is superficial:**

- The blisters are **thin-roofed** and rupture easily
- It is **highly contagious** — the organism is right on the surface
- It usually **heals without scarring**
- **Topical** treatment often works

---

## Step 2: Why the honey-coloured crust?

Follow it through:

**1.** Superficial blisters form in the epidermis

**2.** They **rupture easily** — nothing thick covering them

**3.** Yellow **exudate** escapes

**4.** It **dries** on the skin

**5.** The result is a **golden, honey-coloured crust**

---

>> That crust is the signature of impetigo. If you see honey-coloured crusting around a child's nose and mouth, you have the diagnosis.

---

## Step 3: Why so contagious?

>> Transmitted by **direct contact with infected persons or fomites**.

**Fomites** = inanimate objects that carry infection — towels, clothing, bedding, toys.

---

And the lecturer adds:

>> The infection may spread to other parts of the body **by the fingers, clothing, and towels**.

**So the child re-infects himself** — scratches the crust, then touches somewhere else.

This is why impetigo spreads through families, schools and crowded homes.

---

## Step 4: Primary versus secondary

**Primary impetigo** — more common in **children**. Infection through **minor breaks** in the skin.

**Secondary impetigo** — occurs at **any age**, as secondary infection of **trauma or wounds**.

>> Primary = the impetigo came first. Secondary = something else broke the skin first, and impetigo moved in.

---

## Step 5: The two classic types — get this table cold

| | **BULLOUS** | **NON-BULLOUS** |
|---|---|---|
| Organism | **_S. aureus_ phage group 2** — ALWAYS | *S. aureus* or *S. pyogenes* or mixed |
| Skin required | **Intact** skin | **Trauma** sites |
| Lesion | Flaccid transparent **bullae**, clear yellow or turbid fluid | 1–3 cm lesions, **honey crust**, surrounding erythema |
| Site | Face, buttocks, trunk, perineum, extremities | Mostly **around mouth and nose** |
| Age | **Neonates and under 5s** | Any |
| Frequency | Less common | **More common** |
| Systemic | **Systemic symptoms common** | Lymphadenopathy |

---

## Step 6: The two details that unlock the table

---

**Detail 1 — Bullous impetigo develops on INTACT skin.**

Why is that possible, when every other infection needs a breach?

>> Because *S. aureus* phage group 2 produces a **toxin** that splits the epidermis from within. It does not need a door — it makes one.

**This is the same phage group and the same toxin family that causes SSSS.**

>> Think of bullous impetigo as **localised SSSS**, and SSSS as **generalised bullous impetigo**. Same mechanism, different scale.

---

**Detail 2 — Non-bullous impetigo affects trauma sites.**

It has no toxin to open the skin, so it needs the skin already broken.

>> Hence the predisposing lesions: **insect bites, abrasions, lacerations, chickenpox, scabies, pediculosis, burns.**

Notice they are all either **itchy** (so scratched) or **already broken**.

---

## Step 7: How is it diagnosed?

>> **Clinical** — but can be confirmed by **gram stain or culture** of crust or fluid from bullae.

You rarely need the laboratory. The honey crust is enough.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Bullous** = toxin makes its own door = intact skin = under-5s = systemic symptoms.

>> **Non-bullous** = needs a door already open = trauma sites = commoner = honey crust around the mouth.
cards.tie:
The bullous/SSSS link is worth holding on to.

One toxin-producing organism, producing localised blisters in one case and stripping the whole skin in the other.

cards.q: How is impetigo treated, and why is each step used?
cards.lecturer: Local infections are treated with topical saline or aluminium acetate, then 2% mupirocin ointment. Normal saline or potassium permanganate soaks will help remove the crusts. Mupirocin ointment or fusidic acid as a topical antibiotic. If systemic symptoms are present, use cloxacillin or a beta-lactamase resistant antibiotic e.g. cephalexin. If impetigo is due to MRSA, use clindamycin.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why must the crust come off FIRST?

This is the step students skip, and it is the reason treatment fails.

---

**Think about what the crust is.**

Dried exudate — a hard, thick shell sitting on top of the infected epidermis.

---

**So if you apply mupirocin ointment onto the crust:**

- The ointment sits **on the crust**
- The bacteria are **underneath it**
- The drug never reaches them

---

>> **The crust is a roof over the infection.** Remove the roof, and the antibiotic reaches the enemy.

That is why the lecturer says: saline or aluminium acetate **first**, *then* 2% mupirocin.

**Order matters.**

---

## Step 2: What removes the crusts?

**Normal saline soaks** — gentle, physiological, softens dried exudate so it lifts away without tearing skin.

**Aluminium acetate** — an astringent; dries and soothes weeping lesions.

**Potassium permanganate soaks** — mildly antiseptic as well as crust-lifting.

---

>> **Soak, don't scrub.** Ripping crusts off tears the epidermis and creates new sites for infection.

---

## Step 3: The topical antibiotics

**Mupirocin 2%** — the first choice. Excellent against *S. aureus* and *S. pyogenes*, and used topically so systemic side effects are minimal.

**Fusidic acid** — the alternative.

---

**Why is topical treatment enough at all?**

>> Because impetigo is confined to the **epidermis**. A topical drug can physically reach the whole depth of the infection.

Compare with a furuncle in subcutaneous tissue, where topical treatment would be pointless.

---

## Step 4: When do you go systemic?

>> **"If systemic symptoms present."**

That is your trigger — fever, malaise, an unwell child, or extensive disease.

---

**Remember from the previous card:** systemic symptoms are **common in bullous impetigo**.

>> So bullous impetigo in a neonate is far more likely to need oral antibiotics than a few crusts around a 6-year-old's nose.

---

## Step 5: Why cloxacillin, and what does 'beta-lactamase resistant' mean?

Follow the reasoning:

---

**Step 1:** *S. aureus* produces an enzyme called **beta-lactamase** (penicillinase).

**Step 2:** This enzyme **destroys the beta-lactam ring** — the working part of penicillin.

**Step 3:** So ordinary penicillin is **chewed up before it can act**.

**Step 4:** **Cloxacillin** is chemically modified so the enzyme cannot attack it.

---

>> That is what "beta-lactamase resistant" means: the **drug** resists the enzyme — not the bacterium resisting the drug.

**Cephalexin** (a cephalosporin) is also stable against it, which is why it is the named alternative.

---

## Step 6: And if it is MRSA?

**MRSA** = Methicillin-Resistant *Staphylococcus aureus*.

>> MRSA has changed the **target** the drug binds to, not just the enzyme. So **all** beta-lactams fail — cloxacillin and cephalexin included.

**Use clindamycin** — a completely different class.

---

## Step 7: Do not forget the public health part

Impetigo is **contagious** and spreads by **fingers, clothing and towels**.

---

>> So treatment must include: separate towels, cut fingernails short, wash hands, and keep the child from close contact until treated.

Otherwise you cure this child and infect the next.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Lift the crust → apply the antibiotic → go systemic only if the child is systemically unwell → remember MRSA → and stop it spreading.**
cards.tie:
The 'remove the crust first' principle applies far beyond impetigo.

Any topical treatment must physically reach the organism, or it is decoration.

cards.q: What is cellulitis, what predisposes to it, and what are its complications?
cards.lecturer: Cellulitis is a serious bacterial skin infection that affects the deeper layers of the skin and underlying tissues. It occurs when bacteria normally found on the skin surface breach the protective barrier. It appears as a painful, red, swollen and warm area that can spread quickly. It is primarily caused by Staphylococcus or Streptococcus bacteria entering through a break in the skin. Predisposing factors: skin injuries (cuts, surgical wounds, insect bites, tattoos or piercings); pre-existing skin conditions (eczema, athlete's foot, psoriasis); weakened immune system (diabetes, HIV, immunosuppressant medications); poor circulation (lymphoedema, obesity). Clinical features: redness and swelling of the skin; pain or tenderness to touch; a feeling of warmth in the affected area; blisters, fever or swollen lymph nodes in severe cases. Complications: bacteraemia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, suppurative arthritis, and thrombophlebitis.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: What makes cellulitis different from everything else so far?

**Depth, and the absence of a boundary.**

---

- Impetigo → epidermis
- Folliculitis → one follicle
- Furuncle → one follicle, subcutaneous
- Carbuncle → a group of follicles

>> **Cellulitis is not confined to a follicle at all.** It spreads through the deeper layers and underlying tissue, in every direction.

That is why the lecturer says it **"can spread quickly"** — nothing is containing it.

---

## Step 2: The four cardinal features are just inflammation

- **Redness** — vasodilation bringing blood to the area
- **Swelling** — fluid leaking from dilated vessels
- **Warmth** — increased blood flow
- **Pain/tenderness** — inflammatory mediators on nerve endings

---

>> These are the classic signs of inflammation: *rubor, tumor, calor, dolor*. You already know them.

**In severe cases:** blisters, fever, swollen lymph nodes.

>> Fever and lymphadenopathy mean it is no longer local. The infection is being carried away in lymph.

---

## Step 3: The risk factors — four routes to the same result

---

**Route 1 — Something breaks the skin**

Cuts, surgical wounds, insect bites, **tattoos**, **piercings**.

>> Tattoos and piercings are worth noting: they are **deliberate**, repeated breaches, often done without sterile technique.

---

**Route 2 — Skin is already abnormal**

Eczema, **athlete's foot**, psoriasis.

>> Athlete's foot deserves special attention. Fungal infection **fissures the skin between the toes**, and those cracks are a classic entry point for the bacteria that cause leg cellulitis.

This connects the two skin lectures: **treating a fungal infection can prevent a bacterial one.**

---

**Route 3 — Immunity is weak**

Diabetes, HIV, immunosuppressants.

>> Diabetes is doubly bad — poor immunity **and** poor circulation.

---

**Route 4 — Circulation is poor**

**Lymphoedema**, obesity.

**Why does poor circulation matter so much?**

- White cells and antibodies arrive **in blood** — poor flow means poor delivery
- Antibiotics also arrive **in blood** — so drugs reach the site poorly too
- Stagnant tissue fluid is a **culture medium**

>> And there is a vicious cycle: cellulitis damages lymphatics → worsens lymphoedema → predisposes to more cellulitis.

---

## Step 4: The complications — understand the route each one takes

This is where cellulitis stops being a skin problem.

---

**Bacteraemia** — bacteria enter the **bloodstream**.

>> This is the gateway. Every complication below follows from it.

---

**Endocarditis** — the bacteria in the blood settle on **heart valves**.

>> *S. aureus* is notorious for attacking even normal valves and destroying them rapidly.

---

**Osteomyelitis** — infection of **bone**, either by direct spread from adjacent tissue or via blood.

---

**Suppurative arthritis** — infection of a **joint**.

>> A joint is a closed space. Pus inside it destroys cartilage within days, causing permanent damage. In a child, this can affect a growth plate and limb growth.

---

**Thrombophlebitis** — inflammation of a **vein with clot formation**.

>> Inflammation extends to the vein wall → the wall is damaged → blood clots there. That clot can then break off.

---

## Putting it all together

>> Cellulitis is not a rash. It is a **spreading, deep infection with a direct route into the bloodstream**, and from there to heart, bone, joints and veins.

That is why it is called **serious** in the very first line of the definition.
cards.tie:
Note how the risk factors map onto the eight defences from the first card.

Cellulitis is what happens when the barrier fails **and** the host cannot compensate.

cards.q: How is cellulitis treated, and why does each measure help?
cards.lecturer: Oral and IV antibiotics: first-line antibiotic treatments should target common bacteria like Streptococcus and Staphylococcus. Wound care and drainage: any open wounds or cuts should be kept clean and covered to help speed up healing. Elevation and rest: resting the affected area and elevating it above the level of the heart reduces swelling and improves blood flow. Pain management: analgesics such as ibuprofen or paracetamol are often recommended to reduce pain and fever.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why antibiotics must be systemic, never topical

Cellulitis is in the **deeper layers and underlying tissues**.

---

>> A cream applied to the surface **cannot reach** deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue. It would be like treating pneumonia with a chest rub.

So: **oral or IV**. Never topical alone.

---

**Oral or IV — how do you decide?**

- **Oral** — localised, no fever, well child, reliable follow-up
- **IV** — fever, rapid spread, systemically unwell, failure of oral therapy, or the very young

---

## Step 2: Why target Strep AND Staph?

Because the lecturer names both as causes, and you cannot tell which one it is by looking.

---

>> **You treat before you know.** Blood cultures are positive in only a minority of cellulitis cases, and waiting for them means letting the infection spread.

So first-line therapy must cover **both** organisms — this is **empirical therapy**.

---

## Step 3: Wound care and drainage

>> "Any open wounds or cuts should be kept **clean and covered**."

**Two purposes:**

**1. Stop more bacteria getting in** — the wound was the entry point in the first place, and it is still open.

**2. Stop bacteria getting out** — protecting others, and preventing the child from spreading it elsewhere on his own body.

---

**And drainage:** if pus has collected, it must be let out.

>> Same principle as the furuncle: **antibiotics cannot penetrate a collection of pus**, because pus has no blood supply.

---

## Step 4: Elevation — a mechanical treatment, and genuinely effective

>> "Elevating it **above the level of the heart** reduces swelling and improves blood flow."

**Why does the height matter?**

---

**Step 1:** Venous and lymphatic return from a limb works **against gravity**.

**Step 2:** A swollen, inflamed limb hanging down accumulates fluid — gravity pulls it in and nothing pushes it out.

**Step 3:** Raise it **above the heart**, and gravity now works **for** you — fluid drains back towards the trunk.

---

**What does that achieve?**

- **Less swelling** → less pressure on small vessels → better arterial inflow
- **Better inflow** → more white cells, more antibiotic delivered
- **Less pain**, because tension in the tissue falls

---

>> Elevation is not comfort care. It **increases the concentration of antibiotic reaching the infected tissue.**

And note the contrast with the previous card: **poor circulation is a risk factor**. Elevation is you correcting that risk factor directly.

---

## Step 5: Rest

Movement of an inflamed limb:

- Increases pain
- **Pumps organisms into lymphatics**, helping spread

>> Rest limits the spread. Elevation drains the swelling. Together they are one intervention.

---

## Step 6: Analgesia — and one useful nuance

**Ibuprofen or paracetamol** — reduce **pain and fever**.

---

**Why does pain relief matter medically, not just humanely?**

- A child in pain will not rest or allow elevation
- Fever increases fluid losses and distress

---

>> **Ibuprofen** is an NSAID, so it also reduces **inflammation** — helping the swelling as well as the pain.

---

## Step 7: What you must watch for

Go back to the complications list.

>> If fever persists, the area keeps spreading despite antibiotics, or the child becomes systemically unwell — think **bacteraemia**, and think about the joint, the bone and the heart.

Mark the edge of the redness with a pen so you can see objectively whether it is advancing.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Systemic antibiotics covering both organisms · drain any pus · close the entry wound · elevate above the heart · rest · relieve pain.**

Four of those six are not drugs at all.
cards.tie:
Elevation is the clearest example in this lecture of **understanding beating memorising**.

'Elevate the limb' sounds like nursing advice until you realise it is a way of getting more antibiotic into infected tissue.

cards.q: What is staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, and why is it different from every other infection in this lecture?
cards.lecturer: SSSS, or Ritter's disease, is caused by exfoliative toxins A and B produced by Staphylococcus aureus belonging to phage group B. Scarlatiniform erythema develops diffusely with a predilection for flexural and periorificial areas. Development of extensive superficial sterile flaccid blisters or bullae occurs, which become deroofed revealing large areas of raw red moist skin similar to a hot water burn. Fever, rhinitis, conjunctivitis, irritability, malaise, poor feeding and exquisite tenderness may occur prior to onset of rash. Circumpolar erythema, radial crusting and fissuring around the eyes, mouth and nose are characteristically prominent. The child may appear toxic. Nikolsky's sign (separation of areas of epidermis in response to shearing pressure on skin) is positive. Treatment: antibiotics e.g. flucloxacillin, erythromycin; analgesics; application of an emollient, clean with isotonic saline; correct fluid and electrolyte balance.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The one word that changes everything — STERILE

Read the description again:

>> "Extensive superficial **sterile** flaccid blisters or bullae."

**Sterile means there are no bacteria in the blisters.**

---

**So how can this be a staphylococcal infection?**

Because it is not an infection of the skin at all.

>> The *S. aureus* is sitting somewhere else entirely — a throat, a conjunctiva, an umbilicus, a small wound. It produces **exfoliative toxins A and B**, which travel in the **bloodstream** and strip the skin from a distance.

---

>> **This is a toxin disease, not a skin infection.** Every other condition in this lecture is bacteria in the skin. This one is poison in the blood.

---

## Step 2: What does the toxin actually do?

The exfoliative toxins split the epidermis at a **very superficial level** — near the top of the epidermis.

---

**Three consequences follow directly:**

**1. The blisters are FLACCID (floppy), not tense.**

Because the roof is so thin it cannot hold pressure.

**2. They deroof easily**, leaving raw, red, moist skin.

**3. It heals WITHOUT scarring.**

>> The split is above the dermis, and scarring only happens when the dermis is damaged. This is genuinely good news for the child.

---

## Step 3: Nikolsky's sign — and why it is positive here

>> **Separation of areas of epidermis in response to shearing pressure on the skin.**

You press and slide your finger sideways on apparently *normal* skin — and the top layer slides off.

---

**Why?**

>> Because the toxin has already split the epidermis **everywhere**, including skin that still looks intact. The layer is sitting there unglued, waiting for the smallest force.

This is a bedside test with no equipment, and it is close to diagnostic.

---

## Step 4: The distribution tells you it came from the blood

**Flexural and periorificial areas.**

- **Flexural** = skin creases — neck, axillae, groin
- **Periorificial** = around the openings — eyes, mouth, nose

---

>> And the classic picture: **circumpolar erythema, radial crusting and fissuring around the eyes, mouth and nose.**

Radial = the cracks **radiate outwards** from the mouth like spokes.

---

>> Notice that this distribution is **symmetrical and widespread** — exactly what you expect from something arriving via blood, and nothing like the localised patch of a cellulitis.

---

## Step 5: The prodrome — the child is ill BEFORE the rash

>> Fever, rhinitis, conjunctivitis, irritability, malaise, poor feeding and **exquisite tenderness** occur **prior to the onset of rash**.

---

**Two things worth noticing:**

**"Exquisite tenderness"** — the skin hurts to touch before anything is visible. A baby who screams when picked up, with no rash yet, may be in the prodrome.

**Rhinitis and conjunctivitis** — these point to where the *S. aureus* may actually be living: the nose and the eyes.

---

## Step 6: Why is treatment about FLUID as much as antibiotics?

The key phrase: **"similar to a hot water burn."**

---

>> Take that comparison seriously. Manage the child as you would a burn.

**A child with no skin loses:**

- **Water** — evaporating from the raw surface
- **Electrolytes** — lost in the exudate
- **Heat** — no barrier, so hypothermia
- **Protection** — the raw surface can be secondarily infected

---

>> This is why *"correct fluid and electrolyte balance"* is in the treatment list. **Children die of SSSS from fluid loss, not from the staphylococcus.**

---

## Step 7: The rest of the treatment

**Antibiotics — flucloxacillin, erythromycin**

>> These do not treat the skin. They kill the *S. aureus* **at the hidden source**, so the toxin factory shuts down. Flucloxacillin is beta-lactamase resistant; erythromycin is the alternative.

**Analgesics** — the skin is exquisitely tender and this is genuinely painful.

**Emollient, cleaned with isotonic saline** — protects the raw surface and reduces evaporative loss. Isotonic saline because it does not damage exposed tissue.

---

## Step 8: Link it back to bullous impetigo

>> Bullous impetigo — ***S. aureus* phage group 2**, toxin, blisters on intact skin, **localised**.

>> SSSS — ***S. aureus* phage group B**, exfoliative toxins, blisters on intact skin, **generalised**.

**Same idea, two scales.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> A staph infection **somewhere else** makes a toxin that strips the skin **everywhere**. The blisters are sterile. Nikolsky's sign is positive. Treat the child like a burn — and give antibiotics for the source you cannot see.
cards.tie:
SSSS is the reason this lecture ends where it does.

It shows that a skin sign is not always a skin disease — sometimes it is the visible effect of something happening elsewhere in the body.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 1 — A 5-year-old boy is brought with sores around his nose and mouth for 4 days. They began after an insect bite he scratched. There are several 1–2 cm lesions with golden-yellow crusts and surrounding redness, and tender cervical lymph nodes. Two siblings have similar lesions. He is afebrile and well. What is your diagnosis and management?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Non-bullous impetigo.

**Basis:** Age 4–7 years is the typical group; lesions around the nose and mouth are the commonest site; scattered discrete 1–3 cm lesions with honey-coloured crust and surrounding erythema are characteristic; lymphadenopathy occurs in non-bullous impetigo; and it followed a predisposing lesion (insect bite), which is typical since non-bullous impetigo affects trauma sites. Affected siblings confirm the contagious nature, transmitted by direct contact with infected persons or fomites.

**Investigations:** the diagnosis is clinical, but it may be confirmed by gram stain or culture of crust or fluid from the lesions if there is doubt or failure of treatment.

**Management:** remove the crusts first with **normal saline or potassium permanganate soaks**, or topical saline or aluminium acetate; then apply **2% mupirocin ointment** or **fusidic acid** as the topical antibiotic. Systemic antibiotics are **not** required here, as there are no systemic symptoms; reserve cloxacillin or a beta-lactamase resistant antibiotic such as cephalexin for children with systemic symptoms, and clindamycin if MRSA is suspected. Treat the affected siblings. Advise on hygiene: separate towels and clothing, hand washing, and cutting the fingernails short, since the infection spreads by the fingers, clothing and towels.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The crust makes the diagnosis

>> **Golden-yellow / honey-coloured crust** on the face of a young child is impetigo until proven otherwise.

Remember why it forms: superficial epidermal blisters → rupture easily → yellow exudate → dries → honey crust.

---

## Step 2: Which type — bullous or non-bullous?

Run the table.

---

| Feature in this case | Points to |
|---|---|
| **Followed an insect bite** (trauma site) | **Non-bullous** |
| **Crusted lesions**, not flaccid bullae | **Non-bullous** |
| **Around mouth and nose** | **Non-bullous** |
| **Age 5** (not neonate/under-5 typical for bullous) | Non-bullous |
| **Lymphadenopathy** | **Non-bullous** |
| **No systemic symptoms** | Non-bullous (systemic symptoms are common in bullous) |

---

>> Every single feature points the same way. And it is the **commoner** type anyway.

---

## Step 3: Why does the insect bite matter so much?

Because non-bullous impetigo **cannot start on intact skin**.

>> It has no toxin to split the epidermis — unlike bullous impetigo. It needs a door already open.

The insect bite provided the door. The **scratching** widened it and carried bacteria from under the fingernails into it.

This is exactly why insect bites are on the list of predisposing lesions.

---

## Step 4: The siblings are a diagnostic clue, not a coincidence

>> Impetigo is **contagious** — spread by direct contact and by **fomites**.

Shared towels, shared bedding, close play.

---

>> Three affected children in one house tells you the diagnosis **and** tells you the treatment must cover the household.

If you treat only this boy, he will be re-infected within a fortnight.

---

## Step 5: The most commonly failed step — remove the crust first

This is where most students lose the mark.

---

**If you apply mupirocin straight onto the crust:**

- The ointment sits on dried exudate
- The bacteria live **beneath** it
- The drug never reaches them

---

>> **The crust is a roof.** Soak it off with saline or potassium permanganate first, *then* apply mupirocin.

And **soak, do not scrub** — tearing crusts off damages the epidermis and creates fresh entry points.

---

## Step 6: Why NOT give oral antibiotics here?

Because the trigger for systemic treatment is clear:

>> **"If systemic symptoms present."**

---

This boy is **afebrile and well**, with localised lesions.

- The infection is confined to the **epidermis**
- Topical treatment physically reaches the whole depth of it

---

>> Giving oral antibiotics here would be unnecessary exposure, cost and resistance. Restraint is the correct answer, not caution.

**Reserve oral cloxacillin/cephalexin for the child with fever or extensive disease.**

---

## Step 7: Note the lymphadenopathy — and do not over-read it

Tender cervical nodes are listed as a feature of non-bullous impetigo.

>> It means the local lymphatic system is doing its job draining the area — **not** that the child is septic.

Combined with an afebrile, well child, it does not change your management.

---

## Step 8: The hygiene advice IS the treatment

Three specific instructions, each with a reason:

- **Separate towels and clothing** — these are the *fomites*
- **Hand washing** — the fingers are the vehicle
- **Cut the fingernails short** — scratching is how it spreads to new sites and to siblings

---

>> Treat the siblings at the same time. Otherwise you are treating one child in an infected household.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Honey crust + face + child + trauma site + affected siblings = non-bullous impetigo.**

>> Soak the crust off, apply mupirocin, treat the family, fix the hygiene — and do **not** reach for oral antibiotics in a well child.
cards.tie:
This case tests restraint as much as knowledge.

The superficial depth of impetigo is what permits topical-only treatment — and knowing that is what stops you over-treating.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 2 — A 3-week-old neonate is brought with fever, poor feeding and irritability for 2 days, and now has widespread redness with peeling skin. The mother says he screams when handled. There is crusting and fissuring radiating around the mouth and eyes, and large areas of raw, moist red skin in the neck folds and groin. Gentle sideways pressure on normal-looking skin causes the top layer to slide off. What is your diagnosis and management?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (Ritter's disease).

**Basis:** Caused by exfoliative toxins A and B produced by *S. aureus* of phage group B. The prodrome of fever, irritability, poor feeding and **exquisite tenderness** preceded the rash. There is scarlatiniform erythema with predilection for **flexural and periorificial areas** (neck folds, groin, around mouth and eyes), extensive superficial **sterile flaccid bullae** which deroof to reveal large areas of raw red moist skin **like a hot water burn**, and characteristic **circumpolar erythema with radial crusting and fissuring around the eyes, mouth and nose**. **Nikolsky's sign is positive** — separation of areas of epidermis in response to shearing pressure.

**Investigations:** the diagnosis is clinical. Take cultures from the likely **source** of infection — nose, throat, conjunctiva, umbilicus or any wound — since the bullae themselves are sterile. Full blood count, blood culture, electrolytes and urea to assess fluid and electrolyte losses.

**Management:** admit. **Correct fluid and electrolyte balance** — the child must be managed as a burn, since large areas of raw skin lose water, electrolytes and heat. Give antibiotics — **flucloxacillin** or **erythromycin** — to eradicate the *S. aureus* at the source and stop toxin production. Give **analgesics**, as the skin is exquisitely tender. Apply an **emollient** and clean with **isotonic saline**. Maintain a thermoneutral environment, handle minimally, and monitor for secondary infection of the denuded skin.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Nikolsky's sign is the moment the diagnosis is made

>> Gentle sideways pressure on **normal-looking** skin makes the top layer slide off.

That is **Nikolsky's sign**, and it is positive.

---

**Why does it happen?**

The toxin has already split the epidermis **everywhere** — including the skin that still looks perfectly normal.

>> The layer is sitting there unglued, waiting for the smallest shearing force.

No other condition in this lecture does this.

---

## Step 2: The distribution tells you it came through the blood

**Neck folds and groin** = flexural.

**Around mouth and eyes** = periorificial.

---

>> Widespread and symmetrical — nothing like the single localised patch of a cellulitis or a carbuncle.

And the **radial crusting and fissuring** around the mouth — cracks radiating outwards like spokes — is the classic photograph in every textbook.

---

## Step 3: The prodrome was the first clue, and it was there for 2 days

>> Fever, poor feeding, irritability, and **"screams when handled"** — that is *exquisite tenderness* — all came **before** the rash.

---

**This matters clinically:**

A neonate who screams on handling with no visible rash could easily be labelled colic, sepsis, or a fracture.

>> In SSSS the skin hurts before it looks abnormal, because the toxin is already at work in the epidermis.

---

## Step 4: The most important conceptual point — where is the infection?

>> **Not in the skin.**

The blisters are **sterile**. Culturing them will grow nothing.

---

**The *S. aureus* is somewhere else entirely:**

- Nose
- Throat
- Conjunctiva
- **Umbilicus** — highly relevant in a 3-week-old
- Any small wound

---

>> It sits there quietly, producing **exfoliative toxins A and B**, which travel in the blood and strip the skin at a distance.

**So swab the source, not the blisters.** This is the single most commonly missed point in the whole condition.

---

## Step 5: Why is fluid balance listed as treatment for a skin disease?

Because of five words in the lecture: **"similar to a hot water burn."**

>> Take that literally. This child has, functionally, a large partial-thickness burn.

---

**What does skin do that this child has lost?**

- **Holds water in** → he is now evaporating fluid continuously
- **Holds electrolytes in** → lost in the exudate
- **Holds heat in** → he will become **hypothermic**
- **Keeps bacteria out** → the raw surface can be secondarily infected

---

>> **Babies die of SSSS from fluid and electrolyte loss and hypothermia — not from the staphylococcus itself.**

That is why *"correct fluid and electrolyte balance"* sits in the treatment list, and why a thermoneutral environment matters.

---

## Step 6: What are the antibiotics actually doing?

Not treating the skin. The skin has no bacteria in it.

>> **Flucloxacillin** (beta-lactamase resistant, so it survives staphylococcal penicillinase) or **erythromycin** kill the organism at the **hidden source**.

**Shut down the factory, and no new toxin is produced.**

The skin then heals on its own — and importantly, **without scarring**, because the split is superficial, above the dermis.

---

## Step 7: Why handle the baby as little as possible?

Because Nikolsky's sign is positive.

>> **Every time you handle him, more epidermis comes off.** Lifting, turning, adhesive tape, even a firm grip — all remove skin.

Minimal handling is a genuine treatment instruction, not a comfort measure.

And analgesia is essential — this is very painful.

---

## Step 8: Why isotonic saline and an emollient?

**Isotonic saline** — the same salt concentration as body fluid, so it does not damage exposed raw tissue the way water or antiseptics would.

**Emollient** — provides an artificial barrier over the denuded surface, reducing evaporative water loss and easing pain.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Sterile blisters + positive Nikolsky + periorificial radial fissuring + tenderness before the rash = SSSS.**

>> Culture the **source**, not the skin. Give **flucloxacillin** for the source. And manage the child as a **burn**.
cards.tie:
This case makes the essential point of the last card real:

A skin sign is not always a skin disease.

Here the skin is only the **visible effect** of a toxin made somewhere you cannot see.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 3 — A 9-year-old diabetic boy has a painful, hot, red, swollen area on his lower leg that has spread noticeably in 24 hours. He has a fever of 38.7°C and tender inguinal nodes. There is cracked, peeling skin between his toes. What is your diagnosis, how did it start, and how do you manage it?
cards.lecturer:
**Diagnosis:** Cellulitis of the lower leg, with the portal of entry being fissured skin from tinea pedis, in a child with diabetes mellitus.

**Basis:** Cellulitis is a serious bacterial infection of the deeper layers of the skin and underlying tissues, occurring when bacteria normally found on the skin surface breach the protective barrier. It appears as a painful, red, swollen and warm area that can spread quickly, and is primarily caused by *Staphylococcus* or *Streptococcus* entering through a break in the skin. This boy has three predisposing factors: a **pre-existing skin condition (athlete's foot)** providing the entry point; a **weakened immune system (diabetes)**; and impaired healing. Fever and swollen lymph nodes indicate severe disease.

**Investigations:** full blood count, blood culture, random and fasting blood sugar and HbA1c to assess diabetic control, electrolytes urea and creatinine, and swab of any open wound or the toe web spaces.

**Management:** admit. Commence **antibiotics targeting *Streptococcus* and *Staphylococcus*** — intravenously, given the fever, rapid spread and diabetes. Provide **wound care** — keep any open wounds clean and covered. **Elevate the limb above the level of the heart and rest it**, to reduce swelling and improve blood flow. Give **analgesics such as ibuprofen or paracetamol** for pain and fever. **Treat the tinea pedis** with a topical antifungal, or the cellulitis will recur. Optimise diabetic control. Mark the edge of the erythema to monitor progression, and watch for the complications — bacteraemia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, suppurative arthritis and thrombophlebitis.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Recognise cellulitis, and note the word "spread"

**Painful, hot, red, swollen** — the four cardinal signs of inflammation in deep tissue.

---

>> And critically: it **"can spread quickly"**. This one has spread noticeably in 24 hours.

Unlike a boil or a carbuncle, cellulitis is not confined to follicles. Nothing is containing it.

---

**Fever and tender inguinal nodes** tell you it is no longer local — the infection is being carried in lymph.

---

## Step 2: Find the entry point — and it is in the question

>> "Cracked, peeling skin between his toes."

That is **tinea pedis** — athlete's foot.

---

**Why does that matter so much?**

Because cellulitis requires bacteria to **breach the protective barrier**, and the lecturer lists **athlete's foot** explicitly among the pre-existing skin conditions that predispose to it.

---

**Follow the chain:**

**1.** Fungus infects the toe web spaces

**2.** The skin becomes **fissured and macerated**

**3.** *Staph* or *Strep* from the skin surface enter through those cracks

**4.** They travel up the leg in the deeper tissues

**5.** Cellulitis

---

>> **This is the single most important teaching point of the case.** A trivial fungal infection between the toes is the doorway for a serious bacterial infection of the whole leg.

And it connects the two skin lectures: **treating the fungus prevents the bacteria.**

---

## Step 3: Why does the diabetes make this so much worse?

Diabetes appears in the lecturer's list under **weakened immune system** — but it does more than one thing.

---

**1. Impaired immunity** — neutrophils function poorly in hyperglycaemia

**2. Poor circulation** — the other listed risk factor. Fewer white cells and less antibiotic reach the tissue

**3. Poor wound healing** — the entry point stays open longer

**4. Neuropathy** — he may not have felt the athlete's foot becoming a problem

**5. Glucose-rich tissue** — bacteria grow more readily

---

>> Diabetes ticks **two** of the four risk factor categories at once. That is why he needs admitting rather than oral antibiotics at home.

---

## Step 4: Why intravenous rather than oral?

Three reasons together:

- **Fever** — systemic involvement
- **Rapid spread** in 24 hours
- **Diabetes** — impaired delivery and impaired immunity

---

>> In a diabetic with poor perfusion, an oral drug may simply not reach the tissue in adequate concentration. IV gives you certainty.

---

## Step 5: Why cover BOTH Strep and Staph?

Because you cannot tell which it is by looking, and blood cultures are often negative in cellulitis.

>> **You treat empirically, before you know.** Waiting for a result means letting it spread further up the leg.

---

## Step 6: Elevation — a genuine treatment, not comfort

>> **Above the level of the heart.**

**Why the specific height?**

---

**Step 1:** Venous and lymphatic drainage from the leg works **against gravity**

**Step 2:** A swollen limb hanging down accumulates more fluid

**Step 3:** Raise it above the heart and gravity now **drains** it

**Step 4:** Less swelling → less pressure on capillaries → **better arterial inflow**

**Step 5:** Better inflow → **more antibiotic and more white cells delivered**

---

>> In a diabetic whose circulation is already poor, elevation is one of the few things you can do to physically increase drug delivery to the infected tissue.

**Rest** matters too — movement pumps organisms into the lymphatics.

---

## Step 7: The step everyone forgets — treat the fungus

>> If you cure the cellulitis and leave the athlete's foot, **the door stays open.**

He will be back with the same leg in a few months. And each episode damages lymphatics, causing lymphoedema — which is itself a risk factor for the next episode.

>> **A vicious cycle, broken only by treating the fungus.**

---

## Step 8: What are you watching for?

Go to the complications list, and understand the route:

---

**Bacteraemia** — the gateway; everything else follows from it

**Endocarditis** — bacteria settle on heart valves; *S. aureus* destroys even normal valves fast

**Osteomyelitis** — the tibia is directly beneath this infection

**Suppurative arthritis** — pus in a closed joint destroys cartilage within days, and in a child can damage the growth plate

**Thrombophlebitis** — inflammation reaches the vein wall, blood clots there

---

>> **Mark the edge of the redness with a pen.** It converts "I think it's spreading" into objective evidence, and it costs nothing.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Athlete's foot opened the door. Diabetes held it open. Strep or Staph walked through.**

Treat the bacteria, elevate the limb, control the sugar — and treat the fungus, or you will see him again.
cards.tie:
This case shows why the risk factor list is not decoration.

Two of the four categories were present in one boy, and together they turned a trivial toe-web fungus into a limb-threatening infection.

cards.q: KEY CUES — What words and findings should point you to each bacterial skin infection?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating features drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## First — the master idea

>> Most of these are **the same organism at different depths**.

Ask: *how deep, and how wide?* — and the diagnosis usually falls out.

| Depth | Condition |
|---|---|
| Epidermis | **Impetigo** |
| One follicle, superficial | **Folliculitis** |
| One follicle, subcutaneous | **Furuncle** |
| Group of follicles | **Carbuncle** |
| Deep layers + underlying tissue | **Cellulitis** |
| Whole surface, toxin-mediated | **SSSS** |

---

## Impetigo cues

**"Honey-coloured crust" · "golden crust" · "yellow crust"**

→ **IMPETIGO.** Nothing else looks like this.

---

**"Around the nose and mouth" + "child 4–7 years" + "siblings affected"**

→ **Non-bullous impetigo.** More common type.

---

**"Flaccid transparent bullae" + "intact skin" + "neonate or under 5" + "systemic symptoms"**

→ **Bullous impetigo.** Always ***S. aureus* phage group 2**.

---

**"Started after an insect bite / scabies / chickenpox / burn / abrasion"**

→ Predisposing lesion → **non-bullous impetigo** (it needs a break in the skin).

---

## Follicle cues

**"Small pimple with a hair sticking out of the centre"**

→ **FOLLICULITIS**.

---

**"Discharged a central core of necrotic tissue" · "boil" · "healed with a scar"**

→ **FURUNCLE**. Progression: indurated → central necrosis → suppuration → rupture → core discharged → scar.

---

**"Multiple drainage points" + "fever, leukocytosis, bacteraemia"**

→ **CARBUNCLE**. Group of contiguous follicles.

---

**"Obesity · hyperhidrosis · maceration · friction · pre-existing dermatitis"**

→ Predisposing to **furuncles**. (All = moisture or broken surface.)

---

## Cellulitis cues

**"Painful, red, swollen, warm" + "spreading quickly" + "deeper layers"**

→ **CELLULITIS**. *Staph* or *Strep*.

---

**"Athlete's foot" · "eczema" · "psoriasis"** near a red hot limb

→ **The entry point.** Treat it, or the cellulitis recurs.

---

**"Diabetes" · "HIV" · "lymphoedema" · "obesity" · "immunosuppressants"**

→ Weakened immunity or poor circulation → higher risk and worse outcome.

---

**"Fever + swollen lymph nodes + blisters"** with cellulitis

→ **Severe disease.** Think IV antibiotics.

---

## SSSS cues — know these cold

**"Nikolsky's sign positive"** — normal skin slides off with shearing pressure

→ **SSSS.**

---

**"Sterile blisters"**

→ **SSSS** — it is a **TOXIN** disease. The staph is elsewhere. **Culture the source, not the skin.**

---

**"Radial crusting and fissuring around eyes, mouth, nose" + "flexural" + "looks like a hot water burn"**

→ **SSSS.** Exfoliative toxins A and B, ***S. aureus* phage group B**.

---

**"Screams when handled" / "exquisite tenderness" BEFORE any rash**

→ The **prodrome** of SSSS.

---

## Treatment triggers

**"Applied cream on top of the crust"**

→ **WRONG.** Remove the crust first with saline or potassium permanganate soaks, **then** mupirocin.

---

**"Pus has collected" · "fluctuant" · "boil pointing"**

→ **Incision and drainage.** Antibiotics cannot penetrate pus.

---

**"Well child, localised impetigo, no fever"**

→ **Topical only.** Do NOT give oral antibiotics.

---

**"Systemic symptoms with impetigo"**

→ **Cloxacillin** or beta-lactamase resistant e.g. cephalexin.

---

**"MRSA"**

→ Impetigo: **clindamycin.** Carbuncle: **rifampicin, cotrimoxazole.**

---

**"Penicillin allergic"**

→ Cephalosporin, **clindamycin**, or **erythromycin**.

---

**"Cellulitis of the limb"**

→ **Elevate ABOVE THE LEVEL OF THE HEART** + rest. This increases antibiotic delivery.

---

**"Large areas of raw skin"**

→ Manage as a **BURN**. **Correct fluid and electrolyte balance.** This is what kills.

---

## Two facts examiners love

>> **Bullous impetigo develops on INTACT skin** — because the toxin makes its own door.

>> **The blisters in SSSS are STERILE** — because the bacteria are somewhere else entirely.
cards.tie:
This is your pattern-recognition layer.

Recognise the depth, name the condition, then justify it from the mechanism in the other cards.
